steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Build a React single-page Hashi (Bridges) puzzle game with a retro terminal look: black background, green monospace text, no CSS frameworks. Source goes under /app/src/; the test harness serves the production build at /app/dist on http://localhost:3000, so `npm run build` should succeed and you don't need to keep a dev server running.

clicking island A then island B cycles the bridge between them through none → single → double → none. Right-click or Ctrl+click on an existing bridge removes it entirely (single or double, both go to none). Arrow keys move a visible cursor between islands; Enter selects an island, arrow keys move toward another, Enter toggles the bridge; Esc cancels. Invalid moves render a descriptive error inside an element with class error-message, cleared on the next valid move. The error text must be one of: "Cannot connect non-adjacent islands", "Max 2 bridges per pair", "Island would exceed its number", "Bridges would cross".

The board is a single SVG of 800×600 anchored to the viewport top-left — no centering, no page padding. Each grid unit is 40px, so an island at grid (x, y) renders at pixel (x*40, y*40). Islands are SVG circle elements; bridges are line elements (two parallel lines for a double). The keyboard cursor must be clearly visible but must not be a circle — use rect, stroke filter, group transform, anything else — so the board's circle count always equals the island count.

Islands are numbered circles with values 1–8; the number is the exact bridge count required at that island. Bridges are straight horizontal or vertical lines between two islands, max two per pair, never crossing. The puzzle is solved when every island's count is satisfied and all islands form one connected component.

Puzzle data lives at /app/src/data/puzzles.js with three puzzles, selectable via a single <select> dropdown whose options read "Easy", "Medium", and "Hard". Each puzzle exposes an `islands` array of `{ id, x, y, value }` where id is a 0-indexed integer unique within the puzzle, x and y are in grid units, and value is the required bridge count. Easy is the four-island ring at grid (2,2), (6,2), (2,6), (6,6) with every value=2; Medium has exactly five islands; Hard can be denser but must still fit the board. A Reset button clears all bridges on the current puzzle while leaving the islands in place. When the puzzle is solved, render a banner with class victory-banner containing the text SOLVED!.

One thing worth flagging from prototyping: the error-message and victory-banner only need to exist while their condition is active, and the dropdown handler should fully reset bridges when the puzzle changes. The first mouse click on an island is just selection — the toggle runs on the second click against a different island, and once that toggle fires the selection clears so the next click starts a fresh pair. Resist the urge to layer invisible or wider `<line>` elements over the visible bridge for easier click detection: the total `<line>` count is observable and should equal the visible bridge count (one per single, two per double).

Game logic lives at /app/src/utils/hashiLogic.js. Bridges are stored as Map<string, number> keyed by the canonical "x1,y1,x2,y2" string where the endpoint with the smaller (x, then y) sorts first; coordinates in the key are grid units. canAddBridge(islands, bridges, from, to, delta) returning { valid, error } where error, when non-null, is one of the four strings above; computeCurrentCounts(islands, bridges) returning Map<islandId, number> keyed by the island's numeric id (handle id=0 carefully); checkConnectivity(islands, bridges) returning whether every island is reachable from every other; isVictory(islands, bridges) returning whether every island's count equals its value, no bridges cross, and the graph is connected.



Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@f5160cef3cb3:/app#

Analysis: No project files or prior command output are shown; we’re at /app with an empty terminal screen. Need to inspect the repository, then implement the React SPA, puzzle data, SVG rendering, interactions (mouse + keyboard), and Hashi logic utilities. Finally ensure `npm run build` outputs to /app/dist and passes harness expectations.
Plan: 1) Inspect existing project structure and package scripts to confirm React/tooling and build output path. 2) Create/overwrite required source files under /app/src/: puzzles.js, hashiLogic.js, and React components (App + Board). 3) Implement SVG board at 800x600 with 40px grid scaling; render islands as circles and bridges as line(s) without extra invisible hit lines; cursor as non-circle element. 4) Implement mouse interactions: click-select then click another island toggles none→single→double→none; right-click/Ctrl+click removes existing bridge. 5) Implement keyboard navigation and selection/toggle behavior with required error messages and clearing rules. 6) Implement victory detection and banner. 7) Run tests/build: `npm test` if present and `npm run build` to ensure /app/dist is produced.

steps.message:
Analysis: This is a Vite+React app with existing minimal src/App.jsx, src/index.jsx, src/index.css, and src/data/puzzles.js. No hashi logic utils or SVG board yet. Need to implement the full game per spec, ensure build outputs to /app/dist (Vite default) and interactions/error/victory logic work. Also need to add /src/utils/hashiLogic.js and ensure puzzles.js matches required puzzle definitions and labels.
Plan: 1) Inspect current App.jsx, puzzles.js, index.jsx, index.css, vite.config.js to see what to replace. 2) Add /src/utils/hashiLogic.js implementing keying, adjacency, crossing checks, counts, connectivity, victory. 3) Replace App.jsx with full UI: dropdown (Easy/Medium/Hard) that resets bridges on change, Reset button, conditional error-message and victory-banner. 4) Implement SVG board (800x600, top-left anchored) rendering islands as circles and bridges as lines (2 lines for double). Implement mouse: first click selects, second click on different island toggles; selection clears after toggle; right-click or ctrl+click on a bridge removes to none (without adding invisible hit lines). 5) Implement keyboard cursor (rect/highlight, not circle), arrow movement to nearest island in direction, Enter selection/toggle, Esc cancel. 6) Update index.css for retro terminal look (black bg, green monospace) and ensure no centering/padding. 7) Run npm run build and fix any issues.

Analysis: Build succeeded, and puzzles.js was overwritten correctly. However, the here-doc write for src/utils/hashiLogic.js was corrupted (it contains stray fragments like "2, x: a.x };" and "i.x ==>" and shell prompt artifacts). App.jsx and index.css were overwritten but we need to verify they saved cleanly (the output shows App.jsx content interleaved inside the corrupted hashiLogic write, so we must confirm App.jsx is correct on disk). Next step: rewrite hashiLogic.js cleanly, re-check App.jsx, and run build again. Also ensure right-click/cmd behavior and error strings are exactly as specified (they are), and ensure no extra <line> elements are used (we only render visible lines).
Plan: 1) Inspect current src/utils/hashiLogic.js and src/App.jsx to confirm which files are corrupted. 2) Overwrite src/utils/hashiLogic.js with the intended full implementation (clean). 3) Verify src/App.jsx is correct (rewrite if needed). 4) Run `npm run build` again to ensure the project still builds with the fixed util file.

Analysis: Build succeeds. The displayed hashiLogic.js output is truncated and shows some duplicated here-doc echo artifacts in the terminal capture, but the actual file likely starts correctly. Need to confirm the tail of hashiLogic.js is clean (no stray '>' lines) and confirm App.jsx and index.css are the intended full versions. If any corruption remains, rewrite those files cleanly. Once files are verified clean and build passes, task is complete.
Plan: 1) Verify the end of src/utils/hashiLogic.js (tail) to ensure no stray shell artifacts. 2) Verify src/App.jsx and src/index.css contents are correct (full game code and retro styling, SVG anchored). 3) If any corruption is found, overwrite the affected file(s) again. 4) Final `npm run build` to confirm.

Analysis: All key files now look clean on disk: hashiLogic.js ends correctly with no shell artifacts, App.jsx contains the full implementation, and index.css matches the required retro terminal look with no centering/padding and an absolute-positioned 800x600 SVG anchored top-left. `npm run build` already succeeds and outputs to dist/. Remaining check: ensure src/index.jsx imports App.jsx correctly (it does) and that dist is correct. With build passing, the task requirements are implemented.
Plan: Run one final `npm run build` to confirm clean state after file verification, and list dist/ to confirm output. Then mark complete.

steps.message:
Analysis: Production build completes successfully and outputs to /app/dist with expected assets. Source implementation for puzzles, game logic, SVG board rendering, mouse + keyboard interactions, error messaging, reset behavior, and victory banner is in place under /app/src/. No further changes are indicated.
Plan: Mark the task as complete for grading.
